speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Frances Vaughn Fry took place at her home in Kendrick on August 3, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager. After the war, the war began.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: There was two boys over the night.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: After we lived here about a year, I imagine it was or so, we moved up to Cedar Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year would this have been?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: About 18. 18.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That would have been,

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: well I was just three years old, it was 80 years ago. Oh, that would have been 1899, probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was 80 years ago that that happened?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, no. You're 80 years old. I'm 83 years old.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And I was three years old when we went to Cedar Creek. And my life has been right around this area ever since. Well then that would have been, I think, 1899.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And we went up there, we was up there for, I lived up there until my family was practically grown. Then we moved down here again. We farmed up there. And then when my children were, my youngest one was about,

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: when she was passed up, I'd say 12, 14 years old. Then I cooked in camps. Then I took, I helped with all farming on my dad's ranch until I was married. And then after I was married, my husband had a place up there, and we lived there on his mother's place, the Frye place. We lived there for 23 years. And then we moved to Cambridge.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Our older children had graduated from Kendra Gustal, the oldest children. And then I moved down here. I had the three youngest ones still was home with me, and when we moved down here. And then I went out to work. Well, I did nursing. I went with a doctor. I was doctor's helper for many years. And then,

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I worked for anyone that needed help. I had done housework. I'd done anything that they needed. Just worked and went places, you know, to say maybe two or three weeks or something like that at a time. Well, I kept that up for a good many years. And then I got this work that I worked for Mr. Israel, Clem Israel. I worked for him for, oh, I guess 10 or 12 years. And cooking in a logging camp. And I had anywhere, she had a family and I had a family, and she helped me too. And I worked in this logging camp for 10 or 12 years, something like that. And I took my three children with me. I had them with me all the time. And there was three youngest ones. And then I went from there. I came, we were down here. That was when after we come down here. Then I still did nursing and went around different places. I'd help this one out at Harvard's Dine and that one out. And I cooked it for thrashers and anything that was to be had. But I had worked for Mr. Israel an earlier day at the sawmill. And then he went to logging. And I was all up around Clarkie and all up in that country for several years.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Boy, it sounds like you've really had a full life of work.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I have.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I did have.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then after I came, after the children were all grown and gone, I worked for the Forest Service for 12 years. Well, I worked in all the stores around here in Kendrick before that, after we were down here. And I worked anywhere they needed somebody. And I worked for all the store men at that time. The hardware and the grocery stores and the little, just all the little stores around here I worked for most anywhere. And I did that for many years. And then I got a chance to go from there. I was working for Marvin Long over here. I worked for him for, well, I worked for Bluets first up here, Dick Bluets. And then I worked for Marvin Long. And finally the forest man, forestry man, they needed a cook at the forestry department taking these boys, the two kids, you know, the 8, 16, 17, 18 -year -old boys. And I went with them as a crew. Well, I went to every place. I went up. I was on Moscow Mountain. I was over too.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I was up to Klarky.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: To the work camps. Yes.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I didn't know. I was in saw mill, or logging work at Klarky. But the forestry work, I went up to the mountains up here first. And then I went from, just changed from place to place. I was over to Potlatch and I was up to, over to Osaka. Wherever they needed me. And at the time I went to work there. Why then, we had to, if there was a fire, a big fire, come. Why, I was supposed to go with my crew and cook at these fires and everything.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It was on.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But I never did have to go on a big fire. They had a big outfit, that is an outfit that would take care of a big lot of men. Because they had boilers and big kettles and all that stuff, you know, to take care of the crew. And then I worked for them for 12 years. And then when they quit, why, they didn't have then any more cooks on there. But boys would go back and forth. But I cooked for, had those boys. And then I cooked for them and then I cooked for the road man that was working on the road. And anybody that happened to be around with extra, I had a trailer house and all that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And, uh... Well, let me go back in. Let's start at the beginning a little bit. Let me ask you a little about these really early days when you were just a youngster. And you're, uh, why was it that your family decided to move up on Cedar Ridge from...

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, they came from the east up here. My folks, my dad, they wanted a place. And they did think of buying up here right up on the Hill here. And then they decided they got a chance to go up there. And they went up there and, uh, when I was, uh, I was four years old in March. When they moved up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On Cedar Ridge?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: On Cedar Creek. Yeah, Cedar Creek.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, were there, did they take a homestead on the...

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, they bought a, they bought a place up there. It now is the Lion's Place. They, uh, it's changed hands again now. And there's the Lion's Place where we used to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father keep depot work or did he give it up and go to farming?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, my mother and the boys did the work.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: My mother and the oldest boy. And then there was a man that lived close there by us by the name of John Carr. And he helped my mother. And my dad still worked on the railroad. But he'd come home every so often, you know. Oh, sometimes it was sooner and sometimes... Because he went from, well, he was at St. Mary's and St. Joe and, oh, just a lot of those places. He went around and then, of course, he'd come home every once in a while. And, uh, she had this, this man helped her. But she and the boys did the work at home mostly. They, of course, this man helped put in the crops and things. But my mother took care of the gardens and all of that stuff. She did all that herself with the boys' help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh, now, your father, what was the work that he was doing on the railroad at that time?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He was a railroad agent. Yeah, a railroad agent.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That meant he had to travel around. Yeah, whatever they called. He'd get bumped maybe one place and then they'd put him off someplace else. And then he'd have to move again. That is, when they were on the railroad. But then when they come this far then, they were at Crow Agency and, uh, well, back in Nebraska. And, oh, around different places like that. And then they kept coming out this way and then they finally come to Kendrick. But he wasn't the deploy agent here at Kendrick. But he was deploy agent around at other places. St. Mary's and all places like that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But he wasn't deploy agent here. Do you think that it was kind of rough on your mother having to do a lot there by herself without, you know, generally you get the idea that the man did the, you know, took care of the farm again. Well, he didn't.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Because he had this other man, was a very good neighbor. He was a lovely man. He was a bachelor. And, uh, he helped him and my dad work together, you know. And my dad, they, you see, there wasn't money in the country then. And my dad would get the money. He was paid so much. And he got money. And this man would help my dad. Well, they would, it would go from year to year when the year was up, why they would go together and make out what he had done and what he had helped with and all like that. Well, my dad would pay him the money that he figured, you know, that he needed for the things that he had done. But my mother, them, just stayed on the farm and she, well, we raised our, of course we had our garden and our cows and our pigs and all of that stuff like they do on any farm. But she took care of all that herself. She never, she never had anyone but just the boys. The oldest boy was old enough to help her. And then she cleared, I can remember going out with my mother, clearing timber, you know, off of the ground, burning stumps and all of that stuff and piling up and chunking up the fires and all that stuff. I can remember that very plainly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many children did she have at the time?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Just the three. She had two boys later, but they, well, one of them is still alive. My youngest brother. I have just one brother left is all. I have four brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the kids, like did you start helping her at an early age when you were still young?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Not particularly, no.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I never had people in my life.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I wasn't, of course I wasn't old enough to do very much even though I could, but I didn't help her very much. But for that, the first, oh, I worked out a little bit before I was married, but then not very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that what your mother was doing was unusual for what the average woman did in those days on the place?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I don't think she was doing anything average. Our neighbors was all the same way, although the man was home with the neighbors. My mother's husband was gone, you see, all that time. But then that was between them that she was willing to stay there and do these things because she could do it. And she had these, you know, we had sold eggs and butter and all of that stuff along with the other things. She didn't seem to think that she was work too. But she could do most of that, she could do all that by herself. She really didn't need to have a man do it for her. No, no, there was no man to do it. She had to do it because her dad was gone all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had she grown up on a farm?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I don't know if she had necessarily grown up on very much of a farm. Our folks lived in, let's see, in Iowa, I believe it was. But I don't think she had, she worked, I think she worked out more than, than worked out, you know, went to different homes and worked instead of doing other work. Until she was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did your parents meet? Do you know?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, my dad was from Missouri. And I really don't know where they met. But I imagine my mother was from Iowa.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But I don't. Yeah, well, it doesn't matter. I'm just thinking where they came from, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how far from Kendrick was the home place?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Twelve miles from here to up where we lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she go into town very much?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, about once or twice a year. Go, go over to the team, she could drive the team. My brother worked for the horses too, but then mother could do it just the same as any of them. We rode horseback and we had two old horses. One would carry my mother and I and the other one would carry two. Well, the one that mother rode, why he was kind of a, he didn't like to have a second one on him. And he would raise quite a cohort around when we'd get on him. But that was just fun to us kids. We didn't think anything about, we always had a horse or something to ride. And then when we come to town, usually mother would drive to town with a, oh, well just anything, ma 'am, ma 'am. We'd have a car or something. Once in a while, we used to never have any snow to come to town. But once in a while we would get, in February, we'd get snow. And we'd make a trip to town, usually in February. My mother would come down, of course in the fall now, you know that now, I'm ahead of my story. This, in February, we'd come to town and mother would get her canning sugar. It was nearly always the cheapest in February. So she'd make her trips down here in February to get sugar. And I don't remember anything else but sugar. But of course they bought their coffee. Our book was coffee and Lion's coffee and all of that they used to have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe kerosene too?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, kerosene. We had a little store up on the ridge up there. He had a store and he carried all of his things, mostly. He had a pack horse and then those pack boxes on the sides. And he would bring up coal oil and all that and we'd get our coal oil there. Or there mostly was where we'd take our eggs and things. But he'd take the eggs down there. Now he'd gather them eggs up and I guess he took them horseback. I don't remember any other way that he ever took a horseback. And he'd bring those eggs down here then. When we sold them to him up there we'd get our, oh just different little things that we had to have. But we would put in quite a supply. Sometimes we'd send us potain and get a bill of some of the like coffee and macaroni. And all of those extra things we'd send Spokane for an order and get that. And then the other stuff we would buy here in Kendrick. That is the biggest share of it. But we did send for a while. We'd send to Spokane to see what was the place. Bergen's I believe was the name of the store. In Spokane we sent the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they would deliver it by mail?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes. They would mail it down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you use the store on Cedar Creek very much?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, for all little things. And then once in a while he'd get, oh maybe two or three bolts of calico or something like that. You know, we'd get that there. And all the little things we didn't get. Like a sack of sugar. We never thought of getting it up there. But then we did come down here and get our sugar and flour and stuff down here. Those bigger things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much, did you do much visiting when you came into Kendrick?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, we knew everybody in Kendrick.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And, oh, I can't say now who all the people, but this Myron Long. We've known him ever since I was big enough to, well him and I were about the same age, but his folks were here. And they had the store and all. And then Myron carried it on for just, he's been out of the store now for maybe five years. But he had carried it on from his folks that had it. They'd had it at Leland and then they'd had it here. And then he carried it on for just a few years ago and then he had to give it up. And he's just a year younger than I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, did your folks ever say to you or give you an idea of why they finally picked around here to buy a place instead of somewhere else? What there was about Cedar Creek and Kendrick?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I don't know nothing in particular, only they were over in Montana and they were in Kansas and in those different places. You know that sometimes they had a pretty hard time. And that is the people that lived there. And as they come out here, we lived down here for I guess a year, maybe before we went up there. And they looked around and they thought that that was the best looking and it happened to be that somebody, the people that we bought from, why they was wanting to get out and we got a chance to get this place. And they were looking for a place and they finally decided that was it and they liked everything as far as I know nothing in particular, only it was much better than any place that they ever been that they figured that they liked it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Maybe, I guess part of it, the difference is that the crops are a lot surer around here.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I mean you can count on them saying that. Well, we never had such a thing as a failure anywhere around here. You know, and they were always very, well even today life, the crops are just very good anywhere around. There's never been a, we had one wet year, oh that's been a good many years ago. But it didn't, we saved some of it, we saved enough to keep a going on. Well, I don't like some of them, you know, we just wiped out of everything, but we never was. We never went hungry, I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, you say that coming into town just a couple times a year, a year makes me think that it could be kind of lonely out there, but was it or did you have a lot of people?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, no, but then we made our own fun as far as being lonely. No, it was never lonely. We visited back and forth, we'd go and stay all night with one family, you know, go and stay with one, then maybe next time the other family would come over to your place. And we did like that, then we had literary and dancing and popcorn, you know, time we'd all get together and pop popcorn and things like that. No, we made our own, it was never known in those days. We used to come horseback, or I didn't because I was too young, but my brother, my oldest brother, he used to come over to Texas Ridge from Cedar Creek, he'd come over to Texas Ridge to a dance or something like that, he'd come horseback. But, well, we'd go with a sled, if it was sledding time, we'd go with a sled from Cedar Creek over to Southway in a sled, and that was a big deal, we'd get taken up big bobsleds, you know, and everybody go together. And we'd know, we never, and Christmas, my, we had a big time Christmas, Christmas tree, and usually a program and a dance and everything, you know, and we had made our own entertainment. And then later years, different teachers came in there teaching, then they would have plays, you know, different plays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play party games?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, yes, everything, all kinds of party games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a lot of kids that were within a few years of your age in the area?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, there was a bunch my age, and then my mother, well my mother and her friends were the ones that had all these parties and socials, you know, they had these basket socials and hard time parties, you know, everybody dressed like they didn't have anything or something, you know, and oh, just entertainment, we had entertainment, we never thought of going anywhere. I don't know when it was that I, I don't remember what how old I was before I ever saw a picture show or anything like that, like they did. I thought the first time I was, oh, I don't know, I must have been in my 20s sometime, I had a baby or two, and I went to Milton, Oregon to visit my mother. She had moved down there, and I heard my first talking, the lady that she worked for took us over to Walla Walla, and the first, well I thought that was just something out of this world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a talking movie?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I can remember, well what I remember more than anything else was, you saw this picture, this funny picture of those two birds, you know, that they're always around, they're crows, I think they were, and they were always around every place, you know, and they finally got, they were following somebody, going with somebody, and the little one got so he couldn't go, so he hops up on him. And I remember that as well as it was yesterday, and I just thought that was something, that little, was so cute, that I just thought it was just something out of this world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have actually talking in the film, or was it just a silent, it was a talking film?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It was the first time, and it was a, I'll tell you, it was a story, it always stayed in my mind how easy it could be that people very innocently could get into an awful mess and all of getting mixed up with somebody that got killed or something like that, and then they tried to lay it onto somebody, not the one that done it, but they tried to lay it onto them, see, and this was what that picture was about. And it was so easy that everything pointed to this one that they were trying to prosecute, but they didn't get to prosecute him, it came out all right in the long run. But that was the first one that I ever heard, or saw, you know, and it came out in this picture. Hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were growing up, what were the kinds of things that you did that gave you, you know, enjoyment, living in cedars?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I liked riding horses and chasing cattle and all that stuff. I liked to ride, I liked to have, I always had a horse, and I liked to ride and do all that stuff. I did most, the boys, my two brothers, they went away to work, and I did quite a bit of the farm work, you know, plowing and taking care of the horses. I loved the outside, I would lots of other work outside than I would inside to come to work. I liked the outside work and I loved horses.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Always did. I always had ponies, good ponies to ride.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still going to school when you were starting to help with plowing and farm work?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you do that like after you get home from school or would you lay out?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, Saturdays and Sundays, it was a busy time, and steady time I didn't make any difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you using then? Were you using the foot burner?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah. Horses.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I've heard it's not the easiest work to do, using a foot burner plow and getting it to go right. No, but if you're used to it, why it's alright.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: The only thing I couldn't do after I was married, I tried to run the binder but I couldn't make it work. It would, oh I just had trouble with that, but I just, I could do more than anything else. I could drive horses, I drove horses, I could drive two or four or anything like that, but I didn't make it go with the binder.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So at that point you were really, you must have been really of use to your mother for you to be able to do that kind of work.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yeah, well I just grew up with it, I just grew up with it and that was it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, I liked the farm. Yeah, I liked that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay in school there until the eighth grade? Did it go that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then after that, what did you do after you weren't in school anymore? Did you stay at home and help?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, I was married when I was eighteen.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And I had ten children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you meet your husband right there in that area? Was he local?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well he came from California. And his folks come up, they were wanting to get out of town, she was, my grandmother was, her, his mother was a dressmaker. And she was working with that and grandfather, his father was one of these traveling salesmen that he went and drove all of that, when they just had buggies and like that, you know, they drove. They drove all over the country, you know, and took orders and brought in things and they had their, like when they had a hotel here, why they had a sales room and he'd bring his stuff there, you know, and show it in these different places where he'd go. And he was that and she and this boy, he was, he was about twenty I guess, or twenty -one. And him and his brother was with the mother and then they had hired help and they had a ranch and then, or had this ranch and then they had raised cattle and done numerous things like that. But they come in, they had the money to just come in and buy what they wanted.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That was pretty unusual for somebody to have their money. Yes, at that time it was. It was very unusual because anybody that, most of them that had, you know, there's just, the man died several years ago and the woman is up in the home, Moscow. A friend of ours that was raised right there on the ridge when I was and all, why they lived right there all the time.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And so she just had to be, she was put in this, that is, they put her in the home up there. And she's been in there, but she was a lady, she was older than I was. And she lived her life there too, but she's still alive, but there's not very many of us that slept anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you, where did you meet your husband? Do you remember? Do you remember how you met him?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had parties and that's all it was, we just had parties and then we finally decided to get married, so that was it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: My mother was a widow then for a good many years, but she moved in down, my brother, my middle brother lived down at Milton Free Water and she went down there for a good many years. And then my youngest brother lives in Spokane now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you married, did you, where did you move to?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We went and lived with his mother on there, one place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you feel that when you got married then, did you need to have very much to set up a housekeeping?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, we didn't need to have that, we didn't need it, we didn't have to have, well, just a lot of things. That the kids nowadays, my gosh, they have to have it all before they ever get married. But we didn't, we just worked into it as we could and that's all there was to it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And sometimes it seemed like it was pretty hard with the children, so many of them, then we got by with it and they all turned out to be just average kids. And they all went to high school and they've all done pretty well, I've got them all pretty close around. We lost one girl, well we lost a baby and then we lost this girl when she was 13. But otherwise, and I have the rest of my children just all around the country here close enough that we all get together every once in a while. Them and their families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girl that died when she was 13, what did she die of?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: She had what they call lady cancer, sarcoma or lady cancer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were married then, did you do much work besides raising the family in those first years?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, I worked outside in the field.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I helped my husband until my boys were 12 years old. I helped him, I shot grain and done anything there was to do. I took care of most of the cattle, we had milk cows and I sold cream and all that stuff, I took care of all that. And then I would, well, shop the grain and all. And, I don't know, I don't know how I'd done it all, but I did. Yeah, because it just seems like, I'm really interested in this because it seems like so many women would have preferred to stay inside and take care of the house. Now I've known some women who said they liked to get outside and work, but you're probably the first person I've known that really went out and did it. Oh yeah, I worked out, well, I tell you, I worked out until my boys were 12 years old. When they were 12 years old, I said, I'm not going out anymore. Of course, I had a girl, but that time the girls knew how to work. Because every one of my girls can work, they can cook and do everything just better than I can. But we all worked together in that way. And when I went outside to work, I would plan what the girls were to do and they would do the housework and the cooking after they got to be old. I guess ever since they were maybe 6 or 8 years old. Every one of them can cook, even my boys, every boy can cook.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, and then we used to butcher our own meat and I made my soap and we rendered the lard. I rendered just many gallons and gallons of lard and I sold the lard, I made butter, milk cows made butter, I sold cream. And all of that stuff all went along with everything else and my canning. But people won't believe when I tell them what I canned, what I used to can. I never thought of canning. My fruit was all, after the children got any size at all. Well my fruit was all in a half a gallon jar. Well of course, I wouldn't make a taste for it. So I would have to can my fruit and we would get 6 and 700 pounds of tomatoes and peaches and let's see, tomatoes and peaches and prunes. I made just prune butter and apple butter and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: People that make little jars of it now, I made it by the gallons, just by the gallons. Well we had to have it that way, the children all liked it and we liked that when we were just eating that. And then after I was on the farm, my husband used to take our wheat, you know, we took it down to Patahaw. And we'd get our winter's supply of flour and I for a long, long, many, many years, I made one sack of flour. I'd make up in a week's time in bread. We ate that one sack of flour made up in the bread a week's time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how much was in a sack? That would be 49 pounds?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, 50 pound sacks. And I made bread and of course we made cake and all that stuff besides. But the bread was the main thing that I had to use that flour for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now in a week's time?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: In a week's time. Now that's hard to believe but I did it for many, many, many months. Not only weeks but years you might say. I used a 50 pound sack of flour a week. And we would take our wheat down and change it for flour down at Patahaw. There was a mill down there. We took it down there and we'd get our wheat and our oats and our different stuff for mush, you know, and like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, now 50 pound sack, how much of that would go to bread?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, the main part of it went to bread. Yeah, because I would, what wasn't anything I'd, well, usually on Saturdays I baked anywhere from, oh from,

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I won't say for sure, but I baked anywhere from 10 to 12 rows of bread on Saturday. Well, I baked three times a week. So you know what that do. There's no small amount.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then we had meat, we took care of our own meat, we butchered it. And for a long, long time we never canned beef. But finally we got to where we'd butcher beef and there wasn't anything. We butchered after the family got any size at all. We butchered nine hogs at a time. And then cut it up, you know, and took care of it, made it into hams and bacon and then I, well we used to can, I used to can all the pig's feet and, oh, head cheese and all that stuff. I made that up and then I'd add that to eulogy. And we had meats of all kinds of bacon and ham and shoulder all through the summer. We'd smoke it, you know. Then put it in sacks, hang it out. We had a, uh, a granary out in the barn. New granary. And we'd put that in sacks and hang it out there in this granary. It was cool in there all the time. It was a big barn, you know. baby bun. And there's where we hung that meat and kept it all all year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the beef? Did you salt it?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well we didn't for long, no I canned that. When they were, when I got to, finally we got to killing a beef. Later years there was a an old Dutchman that come from Germany. He used to stay at our place quite a little bit. And he was a real butcher. And there was nothing went to waste. When he'd done the butchering there was nothing went to waste. He made everything into the cut the meat and you have, we got barrels, big wooden barrels. And he put his, this, the, oh, hams and shoulders and meat all down in the barrel and made the brine and put over it. And he'd let it go so long and he used to go, he had a homestead over there pretty close to where we lived later years. And he'd come in and go back in the cellar where we kept the barrels. He'd go back in there and he'd just swish his hand down in the barrel like that and come up. And it'd be either it was all right or he had to take it out and hang it up and let it drain and he re -epped that stuff and then put it back on the meat. We did that for years. There wasn't anything. He made everything. He made liverwurst and blood sausage and all kinds of stuff, you know, sausage and stuff like that. And tripe, when we booked your beef then he made the tripe. I don't know whether you know anything about that or not but then we had that too. So we had everything in the meat line to eat like that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He would just be able to tell from running his hand through the, through the brine.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, he could just come right in and just swish his hand down. That's the way you do it. Swish his hand right down in the barrel like that and feel of it and there was a feeling or something that and then if it had to be taken out well then he just took it all out and hung it up in the smokehouse and let it drain while he got this, would reheat this, you know, and everything and get it ready to go.

words: No.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry

When you used to get them down to Lewiston, you'll get them with a 50 pound box or if you went down to, we never went to the orchards very often and got them, but we could get them at times, the end of the week we could get, but then we did go a lot of times too down to before they took them orchards all out along the little Lewiston there. We used to go down there and get them and.

speaker: Sam Schrager

words: Yes.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And you could kind of go with that as far as, that I know it was just, well it was just tables and tables when I was canning it, it was just tables and tables and we had a fruit room on the ranch and I had, oh there was five or six and there was four, they'd go four side and side, four in the shelf, then I just had rows and rows of peaches and pears and tomatoes and then plums and applesauce, you know the fresh applesauce you put a green applesauce and all like that, I just can't. Well just hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of courts, I don't know, just what it'd be. Boy that sounds like an awful lot. And then later years we had the cherry orchard up there too on Cedar Creek and I canned lots of cherries.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh I've often wondered what some of these women, after they got to having the, we used to have the threshers. The men would come in with the teams and bundle wagons and everything, they'd move in on us with the old steam threshers and they'd be there for days, one year we had the beans, it'd come a bad year and they'd be there maybe one day and get the thresher and the next day it'd rain and I had the bean threshers for three weeks and they'd come and go and then the boys would go a lot of times when they were there, it was hunting time, they could go get birds and things. So they'd go get birds and I'd fix the birds you know for their eats and stuff and like that.

Well they just, our house was just a house that they come to and come in we had an extra big long room there that you go back. No, I'm going back. And then they'd come in there and set with this big fire big stove you know big pot -bellied stove they called them I guess, there's a long one. Now go on back. She can't hear but she can see me.

speaker: Sam Schrager

That was at Easter time one year of course it wasn't every year but it was things like that we had big slave parties and stuff like that too.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We just kind of depended on one another that was a way of winning.

What could you do in the case of sickness if you went to somebody's house to try to help them what kind of what kind of could you do I mean they didn't have any medicine of the of the type that the doctors would know that the doctors would give but we'd have different to each household had different things that they use some would use one thing and some of you something else and then we'd do the best we could with what we had and just pass it out whoever was sick but sometimes it was just cause and sometimes it was the mother that is sick and sometimes it was someone else that she needed help and like that and we just help on another.
